unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5! In this unit, you will explore the world of instructional and procedural writing. Procedural texts are everywhere around us. They tell us how to cook a meal, how to play a game, how to build something, and how to get from one place to another. Every time you follow a recipe, read a science experiment, or look at the instructions for a board game, you are reading a procedural text. By the end of this unit, you will be able to read, understand, and write your own clear and well-organised instructions. You will also learn an important grammar pattern called the zero conditional, which is used to describe things that are always true. This unit will strengthen your reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills, all through the lens of instructions and how things work.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Discuss evidence and language choices. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise two to three paragraphs. Present, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners following, testing and improving clear instructions

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To share information or ideas with someone.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Good writers communicate their ideas clearly.
practiceSentences: Good writers communicate their ideas clearly.
Dolphins communicate with each other using clicks and whistles under the water.
How did people communicate across long distances before telephones were invented?
'Good speakers communicate their ideas slowly and clearly,' our teacher reminded us.
When my cousin moved abroad, we learned to communicate through weekly video calls.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - m - m - u - n - i - c - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'communicate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A group of words that someone has said or written.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Sarah shared a quote from her grandmother.
practiceSentences: Sarah shared a quote from her grandmother.
Sami began his speech with a quote about kindness from a famous author.
Which quote from the story shows that the hero was truly brave?
'My favourite quote comes from my grandfather,' said Maya proudly.
I copied an inspiring quote into my notebook so I would not forget it.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: q - u - o - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'quote', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make something seem important by saying it clearly or strongly.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher will emphasize the key points in the lesson.
practiceSentences: The teacher will emphasize the key points in the lesson.
Our teacher used a red marker to emphasize the most important dates.
Why did the author emphasize the storm at the beginning of the chapter?
'Please emphasize the first word when you read the title,' said Miss Rahma.
When I practised my speech, I tried to emphasize the parts about teamwork.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - m - p - h - a - s - i - z - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'emphasize', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To convince someone to do or believe something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She tried to persuade her friend to join the team.
practiceSentences: She tried to persuade her friend to join the team.
Samira wrote a letter to persuade the head teacher to open a reading club.
Could you persuade your parents to let you join the football team?
'I will persuade the class to vote for a garden project,' announced Adam.
It took me a whole week to persuade my brother to lend me his bike.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - e - r - s - u - a - d - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'persuade', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make someone believe that something is true.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I tried to convince my brother that reading is fun.
practiceSentences: I tried to convince my brother that reading is fun.
Theo showed his drawings to convince the judges that he deserved a place.
What evidence would convince you that the story really happened?
'You cannot convince me that spiders make good pets,' laughed Samira.
My sister finally managed to convince me that porridge tastes better with honey.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - n - v - i - n - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'convince', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To support someone or give them confidence.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My teacher encourages me to try my best.
practiceSentences: My teacher encourages me to try my best.
Good coaches encourage their players with kind words, even after a difficult match.
How do you encourage a friend who is nervous about a test?
'I always encourage my little brother to read aloud,' said Maya.
My parents encourage me to practise my poetry recitation every evening.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - c - o - u - r - a - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'encourage', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make something seem bigger or more important than it really is.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: He exaggerated when he said the fish was a metre long!
practiceSentences: He exaggerated when he said the fish was a metre long!
Writers sometimes exaggerate small details to make their stories more exciting.
Did Sami exaggerate when he said his cat was as big as a lion?
'Please don't exaggerate,' sighed Samira, 'the queue was not a mile long.'
I admit I exaggerated a little when I described the size of the wave.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - a - g - g - e - r - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'exaggerate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To think something is true without proof.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Do not assume — always check the facts.
practiceSentences: Do not assume — always check the facts.
Never assume the answer is correct until you have checked your working.
Why do people assume that the biggest player will always win the match?
'Don't assume Maya is absent; she might just be late,' said the teacher.
I used to assume that camels store water in their humps, but I was wrong.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - s - s - u - m - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'assume', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To ask about something to get information.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I will inquire about the homework for tomorrow.
practiceSentences: I will inquire about the homework for tomorrow.
Samira went to the school office to inquire about the missing library book.
Shall we inquire at the community centre about the times for the fair?
'I would like to inquire about the science competition,' said Adam politely.
Yesterday I inquired at the bookshop whether the new comics had arrived.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - q - u - i - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'inquire', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To show or point out something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sign indicates where to go.
practiceSentences: The sign indicates where to go.
Arrows on the museum floor indicate which direction visitors should walk.
What do those dark clouds indicate about this afternoon's weather?
'Raise your hand to indicate that you have finished,' instructed Miss Rahma.
The marks on my growth chart indicate that I have grown three centimetres.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - d - i - c - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'indicate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To find your way through something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We used a map to navigate through the forest.
practiceSentences: We used a map to navigate through the forest.
Sailors once used the stars to navigate across enormous, empty oceans.
Can you navigate to the library using only this hand-drawn map?
'Follow me, because I know how to navigate these crowded corridors,' grinned Theo.
On our trip, I helped my father navigate by reading the road signs.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - a - v - i - g - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'navigate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To pause before doing something because you are unsure.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She hesitated before raising her hand.
practiceSentences: She hesitated before raising her hand.
Good swimmers never hesitate at the pool edge once the whistle blows.
Why did you hesitate before answering such an easy question?
'Do not hesitate to ask for help,' our teacher tells us every morning.
I always hesitate for a moment before diving into cold water.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - s - i - t - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'hesitate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To come out or appear from somewhere.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A butterfly will emerge from its cocoon.
practiceSentences: A butterfly will emerge from its cocoon.
After weeks inside the cocoon, the moth will finally emerge and dry its wings.
What time do the bats emerge from the cave each evening?
'Watch the tunnel, because the train will emerge any second now,' whispered Sami.
I watched a tiny green shoot emerge from the soil in our class garden.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - m - e - r - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'emerge', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A group of people born around the same time.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My grandparents belong to a different generation.
practiceSentences: My grandparents belong to a different generation.
Each generation passes stories and traditions to the children who come next.
Which games did your grandparents' generation play after school?
'Our generation must look after the planet,' declared Samira in her speech.
In my family, every generation has learned to bake bread the same way.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - e - n - e - r - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'generation', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who was forced to work without freedom.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In history, many people were made slaves.
practiceSentences: In history, many people were made slaves.
Long ago, a slave could be bought and sold, which was deeply unjust.
How did Leo, who had once been a slave, show such remarkable courage?
'No person should ever be a slave,' said our history teacher firmly.
I read a true story about a slave who bravely escaped to freedom.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - l - a - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'slave', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A section of text, or a way through a place.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Read this short passage and answer the questions.
practiceSentences: Read this short passage and answer the questions.
The class read a short passage about whales and underlined the key facts.
Which passage in the chapter tells you how the character feels?
'Follow the narrow passage between the walls to reach the courtyard,' said the guide.
I practised reading a difficult passage aloud until it sounded smooth.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - a - s - s - a - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'passage', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The outer edge or border of something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She stood at the fringe of the group.
practiceSentences: She stood at the fringe of the group.
Wildflowers grew along the fringe of the field behind our school.
Why do you always stand at the fringe of the playground instead of joining in?
'Stay away from the fringe of the pond, because the ground is muddy,' warned Dad.
At the book fair, I waited at the fringe of the crowd until it thinned.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - r - i - n - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'fringe', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To come together as a group or team.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The students united to create the Memory Wall.
practiceSentences: The students united to create the Memory Wall.
Celebrations like harvest day unite families who live in different towns.
Can one exciting project unite pupils from every class in the school?
'If we unite, we can clean the whole beach in one morning,' said Adam.
Our two football teams decided to unite and practise together on Saturdays.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: u - n - i - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'unite', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To move from one place to another.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Every year, birds migrate to warmer countries.
practiceSentences: Every year, birds migrate to warmer countries.
Wildebeest migrate across the plains in search of fresh grass and water.
Why do swallows migrate south before the cold weather arrives?
'Some butterflies migrate thousands of kilometres,' explained the museum guide.
My grandparents had to migrate to a new country when they were young.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - i - g - r - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'migrate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To go into a place where you are not wanted.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I did not mean to intrude on your conversation.
practiceSentences: I did not mean to intrude on your conversation.
Polite visitors knock first because they do not want to intrude.
Would it intrude on your game if I sat here to read?
'Sorry to intrude,' said Sami, peeking around the staff room door.
I waited outside because I did not want to intrude on the meeting.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - t - r - u - d - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'intrude', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To gradually become less bright, clear, or strong.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The colours of the painting began to fade.
practiceSentences: The colours of the painting began to fade.
Bright posters fade quickly if they hang in strong sunlight.
Why do the stars seem to fade as the morning light grows?
'Speak up, and don't let your voice fade at the end,' advised the drama teacher.
My favourite blue shirt started to fade after so many washes.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - a - d - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'fade', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To breathe in air through your nose or mouth.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Take a deep breath and inhale slowly.
practiceSentences: Take a deep breath and inhale slowly.
Before diving, swimmers inhale deeply and hold the air in their lungs.
What happens to your ribs when you inhale as much air as possible?
'Inhale slowly through your nose, then breathe out gently,' said the nurse.
I love to inhale the smell of fresh bread in my grandmother's kitchen.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - h - a - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'inhale', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To mix two or more things together.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Combine the flour and sugar in a bowl.
practiceSentences: Combine the flour and sugar in a bowl.
To make green paint, you combine blue and yellow very carefully.
What happens when you combine vinegar with bicarbonate of soda?
'Let's combine our pocket money to buy Mum a gift,' suggested Maya.
For the fruit salad, I combine mango, banana and a squeeze of lemon.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - m - b - i - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'combine', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A specific amount of medicine.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The doctor said to take one dose of medicine each morning.
practiceSentences: The doctor said to take one dose of medicine each morning.
The label says a child's dose is one small spoonful after breakfast.
Did you remember to take your dose of medicine before school?
'Never take an extra dose without asking an adult,' warned the pharmacist.
When I had a cough, Mum measured each dose with a special cup.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - o - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'dose', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The best or most important.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: This is the prime time to study — after breakfast.
practiceSentences: This is the prime time to study — after breakfast.
Early morning is the prime time for watching birds in the garden.
Isn't the front row the prime spot for watching the school play?
'This shelf is prime space for our trophies,' said the coach proudly.
Saturday morning is my prime time for reading comics in my cosy corner.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - i - m - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'prime', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tell Me How.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Read and understand procedural and instructional texts, identifying their key features and structure.
Recognise and use imperative verbs (command words) such as cut, stir, place, and measure in your own writing.
Identify and use time connectives (first, next, then, after that, finally) to sequence steps clearly.
Understand the purpose and layout of a procedural text, including titles, materials lists, numbered steps, and safety tips.
Use the zero conditional (If + present simple, present simple) to express facts and general truths.
Write a complete set of instructions with a clear title, a materials or equipment list, numbered steps, and helpful tips.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Give and follow spoken instructions clearly and accurately in pair and group activities.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use prepositions of position (in, on, under, beside, between, above) to describe where things are placed.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: Tell Me How: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 5! In this unit, you will explore the world of instructional and procedural writing. Procedural texts are everywhere around us. They tell us how to cook a meal, how to play a game, how to build something, and how to get from one place to another. Every time you follow a recipe, read a science experiment, or look at the instructions for a board game, you are reading a procedural text. By the end of this unit, you will be able to read, understand, and write your own clear and well-organised instructions. You will also learn an important grammar pattern called the zero conditional, which is used to describe things that are always true. This unit will strengthen your reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills, all through the lens of instructions and how things work.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand procedural and instructional texts, identifying their key features and structure.
Recognise and use imperative verbs (command words) such as cut, stir, place, and measure in your own writing.
Identify and use time connectives (first, next, then, after that, finally) to sequence steps clearly.
Understand the purpose and layout of a procedural text, including titles, materials lists, numbered steps, and safety tips.
Use the zero conditional (If + present simple, present simple) to express facts and general truths.
Write a complete set of instructions with a clear title, a materials or equipment list, numbered steps, and helpful tips.
Give and follow spoken instructions clearly and accurately in pair and group activities.
Use prepositions of position (in, on, under, beside, between, above) to describe where things are placed.
Evaluate and improve instructional writing by checking for clarity, correct sequencing, and appropriate vocabulary.
Use new vocabulary words accurately in both speech and writing, including words such as combine, indicate, navigate, and emphasize.
The words below will appear throughout this unit. Study them carefully so that you can recognise them in the reading passage, use them in your writing, and feel confident saying them aloud. For each word, read the definition and the example sentence. Try to use at least five of these words in your own sentences before you move on.

readings.title: Tell Me How: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Reading: How to Make Traditional Somali Bariis
The passage below is a procedural text. A procedural text gives you step-by-step instructions on how to do or make something. As you read, pay close attention to the structure of the text: the title, the introduction, the materials list, the numbered steps, and the tips at the end. Also notice the imperative verbs (command words) at the start of many sentences, and the time connectives that link the steps in order. Read the passage at least twice before answering the questions that follow.
How to Make Traditional Somali Bariis (Spiced Rice)
Bariis is one of the most beloved dishes in Somali cooking. It is a fragrant, golden-coloured rice that is seasoned with a blend of spices and often served alongside meat, vegetables, or banana. Families across Somalia and the wider East African region have been making bariis for many generations, and the recipe has been passed down from grandmothers to mothers to daughters. Learning how to make bariis is not just about cooking a meal. It is about connecting with your heritage and sharing something special with the people you love. The instructions below will guide you through the process step by step, so that even if this is your first time in the kitchen, you can create a delicious dish that your whole family will enjoy.
What You Will Need (Ingredients and Equipment)
Before you begin cooking, make sure you have gathered all of the following items. Having everything ready before you start is an important part of following any set of instructions. In cooking, this preparation step is called mise en place, a French phrase that means putting everything in its place.
3 tablespoons of vegetable oil or ghee
Half a teaspoon of turmeric (this gives the rice its golden colour)
1 teaspoon of salt (or to taste)
A medium-sized cooking pot with a lid
A sieve or colander for rinsing the rice

readings.title: The Memory Wall, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
At Umoja Primary School in Nairobi, Year 5 students returned from lunch buzzing with energy. Something was different. Near the library, a long piece of brown paper had been hung on the wall with the words:
“If you remember it, write it.”
Below, there were small passages, each written in a different student’s handwriting. One read:
“The time I got lost and found again at school.”
“When my cousin taught me how to ride a bike.”
“Who made this?” asked Adam, scratching his head.
“I don’t know,” said Sarah, standing near the fringe of the growing group. “But it’s interesting.”
Their teacher, Miss Ibrahim, entered and smiled. “This is our Memory Wall. You’ll all contribute stories this week.”
“What kind of stories?” asked Adam.
“Anything that made you think, feel, or learn,” she said. “Your memories will help us understand our emotions — and our generation’s voice.”
Adam was nervous. “What if I can’t remember anything exciting?”
“If you combine honesty with emotion, it’ll be worth reading,” Miss Ibrahim said. “Start with a moment that surprised you or changed how you think.”
Sarah whispered, “That’s hard. I’d rather draw.”
“You can combine images and words,” said Miss Ibrahim. “Draw and write. Communicate your way.”
That evening, Adam sat at his desk. He thought about his grandfather. The memory came in small pieces — just like the way his grandpa’s voice would slowly fade when he fell asleep telling stories.
“One day, Grandpa told me a story about a boy who was once a slave. He couldn’t read, but he remembered everything he heard. Grandpa said, ‘That boy’s memory was a gift — and so is yours.’”
He reread his lines. Then added:

readings.title: The Memory Wall, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
“I didn’t inhale deeply. I just kept listening until the words stayed with me.”
The next day, Miss Ibrahim led a group discussion.
“If you feel something important, write it,” she said. “If we combine stories and voices, we grow stronger.”
Sarah raised her hand. “I want to include a quote from my grandmother.”
“What’s the quote?” Miss Ibrahim asked.
Sarah smiled. “She said, ‘You don’t need permission to shine.’”
“That’s beautiful,” the teacher replied. “We should emphasize voices like that.”
“Some people might assume stories are only about big things,” said another student.
“But if you navigate a hard feeling, that’s a big thing,” Miss Ibrahim said.
After lunch, students took turns adding to the wall. Some paused or hesitated.
“Don’t hesitate, Adam,” said Sarah. “Yours is good. Honest.”
He smiled and walked over. He pasted his passage next to Sarah’s drawing.
Another student pointed at it and said, “Did your grandfather really say that?”
“That was wise,” the student said. “It made me think about my uncle, who tells stories too.”
That afternoon, Miss Ibrahim held a reflection circle.
“What emerged from our stories?” she asked.
“We’re not so different,” said Sarah. “Everyone has a memory that made them feel scared, happy, or proud.”
Adam added, “If you listen carefully, people say things that stay with you. Even if the words fade, the meaning remains.”
Miss Ibrahim nodded. “That’s the power of communication.”
On Friday, the final day of the Memory Wall, Miss Ibrahim invited a local author to read student work aloud.
He read Sarah’s drawing-story and Adam’s memory. He said, “I’m touched. These words are better than many books I’ve read.”
He paused. “Who wrote this one about the boy remembering everything he hears?”

readings.title: The Memory Wall, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Adam raised his hand. “It’s mine.”
“Did you know that generations ago, stories were passed only by memory? You’ve honored that tradition.”
Adam felt his heart race — but it was not fear. It was pride.
At home that night, Adam’s little brother asked, “What’s a slave?”
Adam thought carefully. “A slave is someone who was forced to work without freedom. But they still had thoughts. Stories. Hope.”
“Yes,” Adam said, smiling. “And now I wrote it down so it won’t be forgotten.”
The next week, the Memory Wall was taken down — but not thrown away. Instead, it was turned into a book.
“Your work will be shared with next year’s students,” said Miss Ibrahim. “You helped build something real.”
“I didn’t expect that,” said Sarah.
“You believed in each other,” said Miss Ibrahim. “You united, didn’t you?”
“Definitely,” said Adam. “We didn’t exaggerate. We just told the truth.”

grammar.title: What is the Zero Conditional?
grammar.explanation:
The zero conditional is a type of conditional sentence that we use to talk about things that are always true. These include scientific facts, rules of nature, and general truths that happen every time a certain condition is met.
We call it the “zero” conditional because there is zero chance that the result will not happen. If the condition is true, the result is always true.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
The zero conditional is a type of conditional sentence that we use to talk about things that are always true. These include scientific facts, rules of nature, and general truths that happen every time a certain condition is met.
We call it the “zero” conditional because there is zero chance that the result will not happen. If the condition is true, the result is always true.
Why Does It Matter?
The zero conditional is especially useful for:
Instructional writing: When you write steps and explain what happens if you follow them.
Scientific explanations: When you describe cause and effect in nature.
Everyday facts: When you state something that is always true.
Rules and warnings: When you explain what happens if someone does (or does not do) something.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply what is the zero conditional? in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Form the Zero Conditional
grammar.explanation:
The Pattern
Important rules to remember:
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
The Pattern
Important rules to remember:
Both clauses use the present simple tense.
You can put the if-clause first or the main clause first. When the if-clause comes first, put a comma after it. When the main clause comes first, you do not need a comma.
You can use when instead of if with the same meaning: When you heat water, it boils.
Reversed Order
These two sentences mean the same thing:
If you mix red and blue, you get purple. (if-clause first, comma needed)
You get purple if you mix red and blue. (main clause first, no comma)
Examples
Scientific Facts
If you heat ice, it melts.
If you drop something, it falls.
If water reaches 100 degrees Celsius, it boils.
If the temperature drops below zero, water freezes.
If you mix vinegar and baking soda, it fizzes.
Everyday Life
If you press the button, the light turns on.
If you do not eat, you feel hungry.
If it rains, the ground gets wet.
If you practise every day, you improve.
If you add sugar to tea, it tastes sweet.
Instructions and Procedures
If you stir the mixture too quickly, it splashes.
If you leave bread in the sun, it dries out.
If you follow the steps carefully, you get good results.
If the jar is not clean, the experiment fails.
If you use warm water, the salt dissolves faster.
From the Story
Can you find these zero conditional sentences in the story “The Memory Wall”?
“If you combine honesty with emotion, it’ll be worth reading.”
“If you feel something important, write it.”
“If we combine stories and voices, we grow stronger.”
“If you navigate a hard feeling, that’s a big thing.”
“If you listen carefully, people say things that stay with you.”
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to form the zero conditional in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Mistake 1: Using 'will' in the zero conditional
Incorrect: If you heat water, it will boil.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Mistake 1: Using 'will' in the zero conditional
Incorrect: If you heat water, it will boil.
Correct: If you heat water, it boils.
Explanation: The zero conditional talks about facts that are always true. We do not need 'will' because there is no uncertainty. Using 'will' changes the sentence to the first conditional, which is about possible future events, not general truths.
Mistake 2: Using the past tense
Incorrect: If you heated water, it boils.
Correct: If you heat water, it boils.
Explanation: The zero conditional uses the present simple in both parts. Do not use the past tense.
Mistake 3: Forgetting the comma
Incorrect: If you drop a glass it breaks.
Correct: If you drop a glass, it breaks.
Explanation: When the if-clause comes first, always put a comma before the main clause.
Mistake 4: Confusing zero and first conditional
Zero conditional (always true): If you heat water, it boils.
First conditional (possible future): If you heat the water, it will be ready for tea.
Tip: Ask yourself: Is this always true, or is it about one specific situation in the future? If it is always true, use the zero conditional (present simple in both parts).
Memory Tip
Exercises
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence with the correct form of the verb in brackets:
If you ______ (mix) flour and water, you ______ (make) dough.
If it ______ (rain) heavily, the river ______ (flood).
If you ______ (not sleep) enough, you ______ (feel) tired.
If the alarm ______ (go) off, we ______ (leave) the building.
If you ______ (combine) sugar and butter, the mixture ______ (become) smooth.
If you ______ (not wear) a coat, you ______ (get) cold.
If we ______ (work) together, we ______ (finish) faster.
If you ______ (press) this button, the door ______ (open).
Exercise 2: Match the Halves
Draw a line to match each if-clause with the correct main clause:
If you water the plants,  ...  a) it melts.
If you heat ice,  ...  b) they grow.
If the sun shines,  ...  c) you get purple.
If you mix red and blue,  ...  d) the room gets bright.
Exercise 3: Rewrite with the Zero Conditional
Rewrite each sentence as a zero conditional:
Water boils at 100 degrees. → If you heat water to 100 degrees, ______.
Plants die without water. → If you ______, ______.
Metal expands when it gets hot. → If ______, ______.
People get sunburnt without sunscreen. → If ______, ______.
Exercise 4: Write Your Own
Write five zero conditional sentences of your own. Try to write about different topics: nature, cooking, school, health, and daily life.
If ______, ______.
If ______, ______.
If ______, ______.
If ______, ______.
If ______, ______.
Exercise 5: Spot the Errors
Each sentence has one mistake. Rewrite it correctly:
If you will add salt to water, it dissolves.
If water freezed, it becomes ice.
If you touched fire it burns you.
If you mix green and yellow you will get a lighter shade.
If you doesn't study, you fails the test.
Exercise 6: Zero or First Conditional?
Read each sentence. Write ZC if it is a zero conditional (always true) or FC if it is a first conditional (possible future):
If it rains tomorrow, I will take an umbrella.
If it rains, the ground gets wet.
If you study hard, you will pass the exam.
If you heat metal, it expands.
If we leave now, we will arrive on time.

grammar.title: Grade 5 Editing and Application
grammar.explanation: Strong writers apply unit grammar while revising complete paragraphs for meaning, accuracy and flow.

grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: changing words without checking whether the paragraph still means the same thing. Fix: explain the purpose of every revision.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: The Instruction Game (Pairs)
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Work with a partner. One person is the instructor and the other is the follower. The instructor thinks of a simple task, such as how to make a paper aeroplane, how to draw a specific shape, or how to arrange objects on a desk. Without showing the follower what the end result should look like, the instructor gives step-by-step spoken instructions. The follower must listen carefully and carry out each step exactly as described. When the task is complete, compare the follower's result with what the instructor intended. Then switch roles and try again with a different task.
Discussion questions after the game:
Were the instructions clear enough? What made them easy or hard to follow?
Did the instructor use imperative verbs and time connectives? Give examples.
What would the instructor do differently next time to make the instructions clearer?
How did the follower feel when a step was confusing? What did they do?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise the instruction game (pairs) and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Explain a Process (Individual Presentation)
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose a process that you know well. It could be how to wash your hands properly, how to make a simple snack, how to braid hair, how to look after a pet, or how to play your favourite game. Prepare a short spoken presentation of two to three minutes in which you explain the process step by step to your classmates or family members. Use clear imperative verbs and time connectives as you speak. You may use small prompt cards with key words to help you remember, but do not read from a full script. After your presentation, ask your audience if they have any questions and whether your instructions were clear enough to follow.
Presentation tips:
Speak slowly and clearly so that your audience can follow each step.
Use your hands to demonstrate actions where possible.
Make eye contact with your audience to keep them engaged.
Use time connectives such as first, next, then, and finally to signal each new step.
End by asking your audience: 'Is there any step you would like me to explain again?'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain a process (individual presentation) and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Interview About Daily Routines (Pairs)
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Work with a partner. Take turns interviewing each other about your daily routines. The interviewer should ask questions like: How do you get ready for school in the morning? What steps do you follow when you prepare for bed at night? How do you organise your school bag? The person being interviewed should answer using time connectives and clear sequencing. The interviewer should take notes and then summarise their partner's routine back to them using reporting language, for example: 'You said that first you wake up at six o'clock, then you wash your face, and after that you eat breakfast.' Check whether the summary is accurate.
Challenge extension: After the interviews, write a short paragraph summarising your partner's routine. Use at least three time connectives and two zero conditional sentences. For example: 'If Samira does not set her alarm, she wakes up late.'
Activity 4: Group Instruction Writing (Small Groups)
Work in groups of three or four. Your group will create a set of spoken instructions for a task that the rest of the class will try to follow. Choose one of these tasks or come up with your own:
How to tidy and organise a messy classroom in five minutes
How to set up a simple obstacle course in the school yard
How to plan and prepare for a class party or celebration
In your group, discuss and agree on the steps. Assign one group member to be the presenter. The presenter will read or speak the instructions aloud while the rest of the class tries to follow them. After the activity, the class should give feedback on whether the instructions were clear, whether any steps were missing, and what could be improved. This activity helps you practise collaborative planning, clear communication, and giving constructive feedback.
Check Your Learning
You have now completed all the sections of Unit 5. Before you move on, take a moment to reflect on what you have learned. Read each statement below and decide honestly whether you can do it confidently, whether you can do it but need more practice, or whether you cannot do it yet. There is no wrong answer here. This is for your own benefit, to help you identify the areas where you are strong and the areas where you might need to review the material again. Be honest with yourself, because that is how you grow as a learner.
For each statement, write Yes, I can do this confidently, I can do this but need more practice, or I need to study this again.
I can read a procedural text and identify its key features, including the title, materials list, numbered steps, and tips.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise interview about daily routines (pairs) and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: Write Your Own Instructions
writing.promptAndInstructions:
In this section, you will plan and write your own procedural text. You have studied the features of instructional writing in the reading passage, and you have practised the zero conditional in the grammar section. Now it is time to bring all of these skills together and create your own set of clear, well-organised instructions. This is one of the most important tasks in this unit, so take your time and work through each stage carefully.
Step 1: Choose Your Topic
Select one of the following topics for your instructional writing. Choose the topic that you know the most about or that interests you the most. If you have a different idea for a set of instructions, you may discuss it with your teacher or AI tutor before starting.
How to Make a Traditional Somali Drink (such as shaah cadays, spiced tea with milk). Write clear instructions so that someone who has never made this drink before could follow your steps and succeed.
How to Organise and Set Up a Classroom Library Display. Write instructions for a group of students who want to create an attractive book display in their classroom. Include steps for sorting books, labelling sections, and arranging the display.
How to Prepare for and Lead a Morning Assembly at School. Write instructions for a student who has been asked to lead the school assembly for the first time. Include steps for planning, practising, and presenting.
Step 2: Plan Your Writing
Before you start writing, fill in the planning frame below. Good writers always plan before they write. Planning helps you organise your thoughts, decide on the order of your steps, and make sure you do not forget any important details.
Planning Frame:
Title: What will you call your instructions? (Write a clear, descriptive title.)
Purpose: Why would someone need these instructions? Who is your audience?
Materials or equipment: List everything the reader will need before they start.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 5 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about write your own instructions.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

quizzes.question: What does 'communicate' mean?
quizzes.options: To convince someone to do or believe something. | To make someone believe that something is true. | To make something seem important by saying it clearly or strongly. | To share information or ideas with someone.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To share information or ideas with someone.
quizzes.explanation: Good writers communicate their ideas clearly.

quizzes.question: What does 'quote' mean?
quizzes.options: A group of words that someone has said or written. | To convince someone to do or believe something. | To make someone believe that something is true. | To support someone or give them confidence.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A group of words that someone has said or written.
quizzes.explanation: Sarah shared a quote from her grandmother.

quizzes.question: What does 'emphasize' mean?
quizzes.options: To make someone believe that something is true. | To make something seem bigger or more important than it really is. | To make something seem important by saying it clearly or strongly. | To support someone or give them confidence.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To make something seem important by saying it clearly or strongly.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher will emphasize the key points in the lesson.

quizzes.question: What does 'persuade' mean?
quizzes.options: To convince someone to do or believe something. | To make something seem bigger or more important than it really is. | To support someone or give them confidence. | To think something is true without proof.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To convince someone to do or believe something.
quizzes.explanation: She tried to persuade her friend to join the team.

quizzes.question: What does 'convince' mean?
quizzes.options: To ask about something to get information. | To make someone believe that something is true. | To make something seem bigger or more important than it really is. | To think something is true without proof.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To make someone believe that something is true.
quizzes.explanation: I tried to convince my brother that reading is fun.

quizzes.question: What does 'encourage' mean?
quizzes.options: To ask about something to get information. | To show or point out something. | To support someone or give them confidence. | To think something is true without proof.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To support someone or give them confidence.
quizzes.explanation: My teacher encourages me to try my best.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Memory Wall, part 1': _____ your way.” That evening, Adam sat at his desk.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Communicate your way.” That evening, Adam sat at his desk.

liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome and retrieval; 10 min teacher model; 15 min guided Grade 5 practice; 10 min partner application; 5 min feedback and next step
liveSessions.afterSession: Improve one piece of work using teacher feedback.

liveSessions.title: Tell Me How: Performance, assessment and reflection
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete the self-paced work connected to performance, assessment and reflection.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome and retrieval; 10 min teacher model; 15 min guided Grade 5 practice; 10 min partner application; 5 min feedback and next step
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the unit quiz and self-assessment.

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Choose any three words from the vocabulary table above.
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you give me two more example sentences for each of these words?'
Then try writing your own sentence for each word and ask the tutor to check them.
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you explain the difference between the zero conditional and the first conditional?'
Then ask: 'Can you give me five zero conditional sentences about cooking or food?'
Challenge: Ask the tutor to give you a mixed quiz with both zero and first conditional sentences, and see if you can tell which is which.
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
After completing Exercise 5, share your five sentences with your AI tutor.
Ask: 'Are these correct zero conditional sentences? Please check the grammar and tell me if any need fixing.'
If the tutor suggests changes, rewrite the sentences and ask for feedback again.
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you are practising alone, you can do these speaking activities with your AI tutor.
For Activity 2, tell the tutor: 'I am going to explain how to [your chosen process]. Please listen and then tell me if my instructions were clear and in the right order.'
For Activity 3, ask the tutor: 'Can you interview me about my daily morning routine? Ask me questions and then summarise what I said.'
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
What is the difference between persuade and convince?
Can you give me more example sentences with these words?
Can you check my sentences and tell me if they are correct?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain navigate used for feelings, not just directions?
What is the noun form of inquire? (Answer: inquiry)
Can you quiz me on these words?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain the two different meanings of passage?
What does fringe mean in different contexts?
Can you help me use these words in a story?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
What is the opposite of fade? (Answer: brighten, intensify)
Can you give me a sentence with dose that is not about medicine?
Can you check my vocabulary sentences?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the vocabulary words?
Can you give me a sentence for each word so I can guess which word fits?
Can you help me write a paragraph using at least five of these words?
Can you explain any words I am still unsure about?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my grammar answers?
Can you explain the difference between the zero and first conditional again?
Can you give me five more zero conditional sentences to practise?
Can you help me find zero conditional sentences in the story?

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: To share information or ideas with someone. Good writers communicate their ideas clearly.
A group of words that someone has said or written. Sarah shared a quote from her grandmother.
To make something seem important by saying it clearly or strongly. The teacher will emphasize the key points in the lesson.
To convince someone to do or believe something. She tried to persuade her friend to join the team.
To make someone believe that something is true. I tried to convince my brother that reading is fun.
To support someone or give them confidence. My teacher encourages me to try my best.
To make something seem bigger or more important than it really is. He exaggerated when he said the fish was a metre long!
To think something is true without proof. Do not assume — always check the facts.
To ask about something to get information. I will inquire about the homework for tomorrow.
To show or point out something. The sign indicates where to go.

selfAssessment.statement: I can read a procedural text and identify its key features, including the title, materials list, numbered steps, and tips.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can explain the difference between a procedural text and a narrative or information text.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can identify and use imperative verbs (command words) in my own instructional writing.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use at least five different time connectives to sequence steps in the correct order.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can explain what the zero conditional is and give at least three examples of my own.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can tell the difference between the zero conditional and the first conditional.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
